name: The Stillhold
descriptionSeed: A walled city of absolute peace — no violence in living memory, no weapons, no raised voices. Beautiful, ordered, and subtly WRONG. What rides with the peace here is FALSEHOOD: the stillness rests on something no one is allowed to say, a load-bearing untruth so old it has become the air. The peace is real and the peace is a prison, and both are true. The counter-image of the Redline — and arguably the more dangerous place, because its foreclosure wears serenity.
encounterFlavor: Serene wrongness — a kindness with a warning under it, a question that makes everyone go quiet, a peace so total it reads as held breath, the slow realization that no one here is allowed to leave the subject alone.
questSeeds: You're welcomed warmly and gently steered away from one question; following it is the whole arc, and the Stillhold will resist you with kindness, not force
Someone inside wants OUT — not of the city, of the silence; helping them speak the unsayable thing might free them or shatter the peace of thousands
The truth the Stillhold is built on is discoverable, and learning it forces the framework's exact question: is a peace built on a buried injustice worth keeping

substrateSource.reason: Sealed and at peace. The Stillhold gave the lattice up as part of the same vow that gave up the war.
substrateSource.radiusNote: `radius` is in legacy map units; `radiusWorld` is RADII on the sphere and is the one mechanics should use. Chosen by sweep against the authored worldPos: at this scale all 26 sources resolve on the correct side of their region baseline and calibration drift stays ~0.05. Larger radii blanket a whole region (a source at ~55% strength everywhere inside it) and renormalization cancels the field flat — the failure CCode measured and reverted.

axisVectorNote: Derived from authored poleIntensity against world_node_atlas.axisOrder (SNG-180). Disposition — what this place IS. Distinct from world position.
worldPosNote: Sealed and at peace. Marches, but its own vow. (SNG-180: authored position — WHERE it sits. Independent of axisVector, which is WHAT it is; a bastion is where the two disagree.)
